questions.question:
Which is the most appropriate match for the items in the first column with the items in the second column?

First Column:
X. Indirect Addressing
Y. Indexed Addressing
Z. Base Register Addressing

Second Column:
I.  Array implementation
II. Writing relocatable code
III. Passing array as parameter

A. (X, III), (Y, I), (Z, II)
B. (X, II), (Y, III), (Z, I)
C. (X, III), (Y, II), (Z, I)
D. (X, I), (Y, III), (Z, II)
questions.to_find: Correct matching of addressing modes to their most natural use cases

understand.plain: This question asks you to match three addressing modes (Indirect, Indexed, Base Register) with three common use cases (Array implementation, Writing relocatable code, Passing array as parameter). Each mode has a primary use case that distinguishes it from the others.

keywords.explain: The instruction holds the address of a memory location that itself contains the effective address of the operand. This is like a pointer — you follow one level of indirection.
keywords.example: MOV R0, @100 means: go to memory[100], read the value there (say 500), then use memory[500] as the operand.

keywords.explain: The effective address is the sum of a base address given in the instruction and the value in an index register. It naturally traverses arrays by incrementing the index.
keywords.example: MOV R0, A[i] computes address = A + i, directly supporting array traversal.

keywords.explain: A base register holds the start address of a program or data segment; the instruction provides a small offset. Used by OS/linker to relocate programs without changing the instruction offsets.
keywords.example: If base register B = 5000, then address 5000+offset is the effective address. Changing B relocates the whole program.

understand.visual_alt: Three addressing modes mapped to use cases: Indirect -> pass array as parameter (pointer), Indexed -> array implementation (index traversal), Base Register -> relocatable code (segment base).

given.aim: Match X=Indirect, Y=Indexed, Z=Base Register with I=Array implementation, II=Relocatable code, III=Passing array as parameter.

terms.meaning: Operand address is obtained via a pointer stored in memory
terms.connects: Passing an array to a function passes a pointer (indirect address), so X matches III.

terms.meaning: Effective address = base + index register value
terms.example: arr[i] where i is in an index register
terms.connects: Naturally implements array indexing, so Y matches I.

terms.meaning: Effective address = base register + constant offset in instruction
terms.connects: Changing the base register value relocates all addresses, supporting relocatable code, so Z matches II.

given.plan: Recall the primary purpose of each addressing mode: Indirect is used for pointer-based parameter passing, Indexed is used for array element access, and Base Register is used by loaders to relocate code segments at load time.

steps.title: Match Indirect Addressing (X)
steps.apply: Indirect addressing works like a pointer: the instruction contains the address of a memory cell that holds the actual operand address. In high-level languages, passing an array to a function passes a pointer (the base address of the array). The callee accesses elements via this indirect pointer. Therefore X (Indirect Addressing) matches III (Passing array as parameter).
steps.note: Think of a C function call: void f(int *arr) — the array is passed as a pointer, which is exactly indirect addressing.

steps.title: Match Indexed Addressing (Y)
steps.apply: Indexed addressing adds an index register to a base address in the instruction, producing the element's address. This is the natural hardware mechanism for accessing A[i], A[i+1], etc. in a loop. Therefore Y (Indexed Addressing) matches I (Array implementation).
steps.note: Array traversal loops increment the index register each iteration, stepping through consecutive elements.

steps.title: Match Base Register Addressing (Z)
steps.apply: Base register addressing separates the program's logical offset (encoded in the instruction) from the physical load address (in the base register). The OS or loader sets the base register to wherever the program is loaded in physical memory. All effective addresses adjust automatically. Therefore Z (Base Register Addressing) matches II (Writing relocatable code).
steps.note: This is the hardware basis of segmentation-based relocation.

steps.title: Identify the matching option
steps.apply: X->III, Y->I, Z->II corresponds to option A: (X, III), (Y, I), (Z, II).
steps.note: All other options misplace at least one pairing.

questions.formula_note: Fundamental properties of indirect, indexed, and base-register addressing modes and their primary use cases in system/compiler design.

questions.question:
In the absolute addressing mode:
A. the operand is inside the instruction
B. the address of the operand is inside the instruction
C. the register containing the address of the operand is specified inside the instruction
D. the location of the operand is implicit
questions.answer: B. the address of the operand is inside the instruction

understand.plain: This question tests the definition of absolute addressing mode. In absolute (also called direct) addressing, the instruction itself contains the actual memory address where the operand resides. You do not need to compute or indirect through anything — the full address is in the instruction.

keywords.explain: The instruction encodes the full memory address of the operand. The processor reads this address from the instruction and directly accesses that memory location.
keywords.example: LOAD R0, 2048 — the operand is stored at memory address 2048, which appears literally in the instruction.

keywords.explain: The operand value itself is embedded in the instruction (not an address). This is different from absolute addressing.
keywords.example: MOV R0, #5 — the number 5 is the operand directly.

understand.visual_alt: Instruction contains address field -> Memory at that address holds operand. Contrast: Immediate has operand in instruction itself; Register Indirect has register holding the address.

given.aim: Identify the correct definition of absolute addressing mode.

terms.meaning: This describes Immediate addressing mode, not absolute.
terms.example: MOV R0, #10 embeds operand 10 in the instruction.

terms.term: Option B — address of operand inside instruction
terms.meaning: This is the definition of Absolute/Direct addressing: the instruction field holds the memory address where the operand is stored.
terms.example: LOAD R0, 500 — address 500 is in the instruction; memory[500] is the operand.

terms.term: Option C — register containing address specified
terms.meaning: This describes Register Indirect (or Register Deferred) addressing mode.
terms.example: LOAD R0, (R1) — R1 holds the address; memory[R1] is the operand.

given.plan: Recall the taxonomy of addressing modes: immediate (operand in instruction), absolute/direct (address in instruction), register indirect (register holds address), implicit (operand location fixed). Match definition to absolute mode.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: Option A (operand in instruction) = Immediate mode. Option C (register with address) = Register Indirect mode. Option D (implicit location) = Implicit/Inherent mode (e.g., accumulator always used). None of these is absolute addressing.
steps.note: Knowing these other modes by name quickly eliminates three options.

steps.title: Confirm Option B
steps.apply: In absolute addressing the instruction contains the literal memory address of the operand. The CPU decodes this address directly and issues a memory read/write to that exact address. This is Option B: 'the address of the operand is inside the instruction'.
steps.note: Absolute addressing produces non-relocatable code because physical addresses are hard-coded in instructions.

solution.result: Option B: the address of the operand is inside the instruction

questions.formula_note: Definition of absolute (direct) addressing: the instruction encodes the full memory address of the operand.

questions.question:
Which of the following addressing modes are suitable for program relocation at run time?
I.   Absolute addressing
II.  Based addressing
III. Relative addressing
IV.  Indirect addressing

A. I and IV
B. I and II
C. II and III
D. I, II and IV
questions.to_find: Which addressing modes support program relocation at run time

understand.plain: When a program is relocated (loaded at a different memory address than it was compiled for), the addressing modes it uses must still produce correct effective addresses without modifying the instruction binary. Modes that compute addresses relative to a base register or relative to the program counter (PC) automatically adjust when the load address changes; absolute addressing does not.

keywords.explain: Moving a program to a different region of memory at load time or run time. Addressing modes that use a base register or PC-relative offset remain valid after relocation; absolute addresses become wrong.
keywords.example: A program compiled to run at 0x1000 is loaded at 0x5000 instead. Absolute addresses like 0x1200 are now wrong, but offset +0x200 from base register still gives 0x5200.

keywords.explain: Effective address = base register + offset. The OS updates the base register to reflect the new load address; all offsets remain correct.
keywords.example: Base = 0x5000, offset = 0x200 => EA = 0x5200. Relocate: Base = 0x8000 => EA = 0x8200.

keywords.explain: Effective address = PC + displacement. Since the PC always points into the relocated program, the relative displacement is unchanged by relocation.
keywords.example: PC = 0x5010, displacement = +8 => EA = 0x5018. After relocation to 0x8010, EA = 0x8018.

understand.visual_alt: Absolute addressing encodes a fixed address — breaks on relocation. Based and relative addressing use offsets from a moveable base or PC — correct after relocation.

given.aim: Identify addressing modes that remain correct when the program is relocated in memory at run time.

terms.meaning: Instruction contains the fixed physical address of the operand.
terms.example: LOAD R, 2000 — if program moves, address 2000 is now wrong.
terms.connects: NOT suitable for relocation — the physical address in the instruction becomes stale.

terms.meaning: EA = base register + constant offset; base register is updated by the loader/OS on relocation.
terms.example: Segment register updated to new load address; all instructions' offsets remain valid.
terms.connects: Suitable — only the base register needs updating.

terms.meaning: EA = PC + signed displacement; displacement is fixed in instruction.
terms.example: Branch +10 from current PC is valid regardless of where program is loaded.
terms.connects: Suitable — PC moves with the program, displacement stays correct.

given.plan: Check each mode: does it use a fixed physical address (breaks on relocation) or a relative offset/base that adjusts automatically? Absolute = breaks. Based = adjusts via base register. Relative = adjusts via PC. Indirect = depends on what the pointer points to; the pointer itself may contain an absolute address.

steps.title: Test Absolute Addressing (I)
steps.apply: The instruction encodes the exact physical address. If the program is relocated, this address no longer points to the correct operand. Absolute addressing is NOT suitable for relocation.
steps.note: This is why old DOS .COM programs (100% absolute) could not be easily relocated.

steps.title: Test Based Addressing (II)
steps.apply: The offset is a small constant relative to a segment base. When the program is relocated, only the base register needs to be updated. All effective addresses shift by the same amount. Based addressing IS suitable for relocation.
steps.note: Hardware segmentation (x86 segment registers) exploits this principle.

steps.title: Test Relative Addressing (III)
steps.apply: The displacement is the difference between the target address and the current PC, a quantity that is invariant under translation of the whole program in memory. Relative addressing IS suitable for relocation.
steps.note: Position-Independent Code (PIC) uses PC-relative addressing throughout for this reason.

steps.title: Test Indirect Addressing (IV)
steps.apply: The instruction points to a memory cell whose content is the operand address. That content (the pointer value) is typically an absolute address. After relocation this stored pointer becomes invalid unless explicitly updated. Indirect addressing is NOT inherently suitable for relocation.
steps.note: Indirect addressing requires the stored pointer to be position-independent (e.g., stored as an offset), which is not the default.

steps.title: Select answer
steps.apply: Suitable modes: II (Based) and III (Relative). This is option C.

questions.formula_note: Based addressing (EA = base_register + offset) and PC-relative addressing (EA = PC + displacement) support program relocation; absolute addressing does not.

questions.question:
Consider a three-word machine instruction:

  ADD  A[R_i],  @B

The first operand (destination) uses indexed addressing mode with R_i as the index register. The second operand (source) uses indirect addressing mode. A and B are memory addresses residing at the second and third words of the instruction, respectively. The first word of the instruction specifies the opcode and the two addressing modes.

During the execution cycle of this instruction, the number of memory references required (excluding the instruction fetch) is:

A. 3
B. 4
C. 5
D. 6
questions.to_find: Number of memory references during execution (excluding instruction fetch) for ADD A[R_i], @B

understand.plain: The instruction ADD A[R_i], @B adds the source operand to the destination. The destination uses indexed addressing: its effective address is A + R_i. The source uses indirect addressing: memory[B] holds the address of the actual operand. We need to count every memory read or write performed while executing the instruction (not counting the 3-word fetch itself).

keywords.explain: Effective address of destination = base address A (fetched from word 2 of instruction) + value in register R_i.
The instruction gives address B (word 3). Memory[B] holds the actual address of the operand. Two memory accesses needed just to get the source value.
keywords.example: B = 2000, memory[2000] = 3000, memory[3000] = 42 => source value is 42.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 640 320' width='640' height='320'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='200' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Word 2: address A</text><rect x='10' y='55' width='200' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Word 3: address B</text><text x='10' y='130' class='sub mut'>Destination path (indexed):</text><text x='10' y='155' class='sub ink'>mem[1]: read A from word 2</text><text x='10' y='175' class='sub ink'>Compute EA_d = A + R_i</text><text x='10' y='195' class='sub ink'>mem[2]: read mem[EA_d]</text><text x='10' y='215' class='sub ink'>mem[3]: write result to mem[EA_d]</text><text x='330' y='130' class='sub mut'>Source path (indirect):</text><text x='330' y='155' class='sub ink'>mem[4]: read B from word 3</text><text x='330' y='175' class='sub ink'>mem[5]: read mem[B] = EA_s</text><text x='330' y='195' class='sub ink'>mem[6]: read mem[EA_s] = src val</text><text x='110' y='270' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Total = 6 memory references</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Instruction word 2 holds A (indexed base), word 3 holds B (indirect pointer). Destination requires: fetch A (1), read dest operand (2), write result (3). Source requires: fetch B (4), read pointer at B (5), read value at that pointer (6). Total = 6.

given.aim: Count memory references during execution of ADD A[R_i], @B (not counting the 3-word instruction fetch).

terms.meaning: The base address for the indexed destination operand.
terms.example: Must be read from memory (word 2) to compute EA_dest = A + R_i.
terms.connects: Contributes 1 memory read (fetch A from word 2 of instruction).

terms.meaning: The address used in the indirect addressing for the source.
terms.example: Must be read from memory (word 3) to obtain B, then memory[B] gives EA_src.
terms.connects: Contributes 1 memory read (fetch B from word 3 of instruction).

terms.meaning: Read memory[B] to get the actual source address, then read memory[that address] to get the operand value.
terms.example: B=2000, mem[2000]=3000, mem[3000]=value. Two reads after fetching B.

given.plan: Trace the execution step by step for both operands. For the destination (indexed): fetch A from instruction memory, compute EA_dest = A+R_i, read current value at EA_dest (for ADD), write result back. For the source (indirect): fetch B from instruction memory, read memory[B] to get EA_src, read memory[EA_src] to get operand. Count all accesses.

steps.title: Instruction fetch (excluded)
steps.apply: The three instruction words are fetched: opcode+modes (word 1), address A (word 2), address B (word 3). These 3 memory accesses are excluded per the question.
steps.note: The question says 'excluding the instruction fetch' — so these 3 do not count.

steps.title: Fetch operand A (word 2) for indexed destination
steps.apply: To compute the effective destination address, the processor must read the value A stored in word 2 of the instruction from memory. This is memory reference #1.
steps.note: Even though word 2 was 'fetched' as part of the instruction, the problem counts reading A as a separate memory reference in the execution phase when computing EA_dest.

steps.title: Read destination value
steps.apply: For an ADD operation, the current value at the destination must be read before adding. Memory reference #2: read memory[EA_dest].
steps.note: This is a read-modify-write operation; the read and write are separate memory references.

steps.title: Fetch operand B (word 3) for indirect source
steps.apply: To resolve the indirect source, the processor reads address B from word 3 of the instruction. Memory reference #3: read B.
steps.note: Symmetrical to fetching A above.

steps.title: Read pointer at B
steps.apply: In indirect addressing, memory[B] contains the effective address of the source operand. Memory reference #4: read memory[B].
steps.note: This is the first level of indirection.

steps.title: Read source operand value
steps.apply: Now read the actual source operand from EA_src. Memory reference #5: read memory[EA_src].
steps.note: This is the second level — we finally have the value to add.

steps.title: Write result to destination
steps.apply: After computing dest_val + src_val, write the result back to the destination address EA_dest = A + R_i. Memory reference #6: write to memory[EA_dest].
steps.note: The write-back completes the ADD operation.

steps.title: Count total
steps.apply: Memory references: (1) read A, (2) read mem[A+R_i], (3) read B, (4) read mem[B], (5) read mem[mem[B]], (6) write mem[A+R_i]. Total = 6.
steps.note: Answer is D. 6.

questions.formula_note: Indexed addressing: EA = base + index (1 address fetch + 1 data access); Indirect addressing: EA = memory[address] (2 accesses to resolve). ADD also requires a read-then-write on the destination.

questions.question:
Match each of the high level language statements given on the left hand side with the most natural addressing mode from those listed on the right hand side.

Left Column:
(1) A[J] = B[J]
(2) while (*A++)
(3) int temp = *z

Right Column:
(a) Indirect addressing
(b) Indexed addressing
(c) Auto increment

A. (1, c), (2, b), (3, a)
B. (1, c), (2, a), (3, b)
C. (1, b), (2, c), (3, a)
D. (1, b), (2, a), (3, c)
questions.to_find: Match each high-level language statement to its most natural addressing mode

understand.plain: The question maps three common C programming patterns to the processor addressing modes that implement them most naturally. Array subscripting maps to indexed addressing, pointer auto-increment in a loop maps to auto-increment addressing, and plain pointer dereferencing maps to indirect addressing.

keywords.explain: EA = base + index. Directly implements A[J] by treating the array base as the address operand and J as the index register.
keywords.example: A[J] = B[J]: for each element, EA_A = &A + J, EA_B = &B + J.

keywords.explain: The register holding the address is automatically incremented after (or before) each access. Implements pointer traversal in loops such as while (*A++).
keywords.example: while (*A++) reads *A then increments A, repeating until *A == 0.

keywords.explain: The operand address is fetched from the location pointed to by the instruction's address field or a register. Implements plain pointer dereference *z.
keywords.example: int temp = *z: read the value stored at the address held in z.

understand.visual_alt: Three C idioms: A[J]=B[J] uses index J as array offset (indexed), *A++ uses pointer that advances each iteration (auto-increment), *z dereferences a pointer once (indirect).

given.aim: Map (1) A[J]=B[J], (2) while(*A++), (3) int temp=*z to addressing modes (a) indirect, (b) indexed, (c) auto-increment.

terms.meaning: Access array elements using index J as an offset from the array base address.
terms.example: In C: arr[3] computes address = base + 3*sizeof(element).
terms.connects: Matches indexed addressing (b): EA = base + index.

terms.meaning: Traverse a pointer A, reading each element and advancing A by 1 after each read, stopping when the value is zero/null.
terms.example: Typical string traversal: while (*p++) processes each character.
terms.connects: Matches auto-increment addressing (c): read then post-increment the pointer.

terms.meaning: Dereference pointer z to read the value it points to — single level of indirection.
terms.example: int *z = &x; int temp = *z; — temp gets the value of x.

given.plan: Identify the dominant hardware addressing primitive for each C idiom: array indexing -> indexed mode, post-increment pointer loop -> auto-increment mode, single pointer dereference -> indirect mode.

steps.title: Match A[J] = B[J] to indexed addressing
steps.apply: Accessing array element A[J] requires computing address = base_of_A + J (scaled by element size). This is precisely indexed addressing where J is the index register. Statement (1) maps to (b) Indexed addressing.
steps.note: Both the source B[J] and destination A[J] use indexed mode; the dominant hardware mechanism is indexed.

steps.title: Match while (*A++) to auto-increment addressing
steps.apply: The C expression *A++ reads the value pointed to by A, then increments A to point to the next element. In hardware, auto-increment addressing reads memory[A] and automatically increments A by the operand size. Statement (2) maps to (c) Auto increment.
steps.note: Auto-increment (and auto-decrement) addressing modes were specifically introduced to speed up such pointer traversal loops, as in PDP-11 architecture.

steps.title: Match int temp = *z to indirect addressing
steps.formula_raw: EA = memory[z]  (z is a pointer variable)
steps.apply: The dereference *z reads the value at the address stored in z. This is one level of indirection: z contains an address, and we access the operand at that address. Statement (3) maps to (a) Indirect addressing.
steps.note: Unlike auto-increment, there is no automatic pointer advance here — just a single indirect access.

steps.title: Identify the answer option
steps.apply: (1)->(b), (2)->(c), (3)->(a) corresponds to option C: (1, b), (2, c), (3, a).

questions.formula_note: Mapping C idioms to addressing modes: array subscript -> indexed (EA = base + index), post-increment pointer loop -> auto-increment, pointer dereference -> indirect (EA = memory[pointer]).
